message:
perf(types): short-circuit Cx::checkpoint fast path

`Cx::checkpoint` was called on every fsqlite operation (hot yield point).
The fast path previously evaluated `maybe_cancel_via_native_cx` unconditionally
*before* testing the cheap `cancel_requested` atomic — so every op paid for
the nested asupersync `Cx::checkpoint()` and its `cancel_reason()` machinery
even when the local bit proved we were not cancelled.

Reorder the fast path so the cheap-first-check wins:

    1. Load `cancel_requested` (relaxed atomic; clear ⇒ not locally cancelled).
    2. Only if clear, consult the e-process oracle.
    3. Only if the oracle doesn't shed, consult the native cx.
    4. Drop through to the masked / slow-path logic otherwise.

Semantically equivalent: previously all three checks fed an `&&`-chained
early return. Now the checks are short-circuited in dependency order so
the expensive ones are skipped when the cheap one already resolves.

Validation (mt-mvcc-bench --threads=8 --rows-per-thread=500 --iters=10,
2026-04-23 19:52 UTC, ts2):

| metric                   | pre-fix | post-fix |
|--------------------------|--------:|---------:|
| `Cx::checkpoint` self-time | 5.87%  | 3.73%    |
| fs_wps @ 1 thread (best)   | 85,604 | 88,837   |
| fs_wps @ 2 threads         |  8,918 |  8,945   |
| fs_wps @ 4 threads         |  5,991 |  5,963   |
| fs_wps @ 8 threads (best)  |  5,043 |  4,918   |

1-thread throughput improves ~3-5%. The 2-thread-and-above ceiling is
unchanged — that bottleneck lives in the MVCC page-lock waiter path
(`wait_for_page_lock_holder_change` / `unregister_waiter`), a separate
target.

Evidence:
- /data/tmp/fsqlite-bench-fix-validation-194151/perf.data (pre-fix)
- /data/tmp/fsqlite-t_cx-validation-195245/perf.data (post-fix)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
